title: API stability policy
text: 1.0.0, kernR adopts strict frozen-API additive evolution — policy glmnet, mgcv, long-lived statistical inference packages: Signatures exported functions never change backwards-incompatible way across major versions. New capability arrives via new entry points (new exports), never via changes existing ones. existing function genuinely needs retired, marked lifecycle::deprecate_warn(), kept ≥ 2 minor versions, promoted lifecycle::deprecate_stop() ≥ 1 minor version, removed next major release. Successors signposted deprecation message. policy chosen kernR provides hypothesis-testing machinery downstream consumers (PESTO ⇄ kernR ⇄ proxymix chain; agricultural pipeline code; methods-paper supplementary material) wire long-lived calibration verdict workflows. Silent breakage across versions defeat contract.

title: Causal Association Testing with bd-HSIC
text: Standard independence tests distinguish causal association confounded association. treatment X outcome Y share common cause Z, appear dependent even X causal effect Y. bd-HSIC test (Hu, Sejdinovic & Evans, 2024) solves testing -null hypothesis: H_0: p(y | (x)) = p*(y) x uses Pearl’s -operator: intervening X, Y still associated X?

title: Causal Association Testing with bd-HSIC
text: Density ratio estimation: Estimate w(x, z) = p*(x) / p(x|z) reweight observational samples interventional distribution. Weighted HSIC: Compute HSIC X Y reweighted (interventional) distribution. Cluster-based permutation: Obtain p-values permuting Y within clusters similar conditional densities p(x|z).

title: Causal Association Testing with bd-HSIC
text: test detects causal association X Y.
code: library(kernR) set.seed(42)  n <- 300 z <- matrix(rnorm(n * 2), n, 2) x <- 0.5 * z[, 1] + rnorm(n)           # X depends on confounder Z y <- 0.8 * x + 0.5 * z[, 2] + rnorm(n) # Y depends causally on X and on Z  result <- bd_hsic_test(x, y, z,   n_permutations = 200,   seed = 1 ) result #>  #>    bd-HSIC Test #>  #> Statistic: 0.0182464  #> P-value:   0.0050  #> N:         150  #> Perms:     200  #> Kernel X:  rbf (bw = 1.062) #> Kernel Y:  rbf (bw = 1.425) #> ESS:       149.7

title: Causal Association Testing with bd-HSIC
text: large p-value correctly indicates causal effect.
code: set.seed(42) n <- 300 z <- matrix(rnorm(n * 2), n, 2) x <- 0.5 * z[, 1] + rnorm(n) y <- 0.5 * z[, 1] + z[, 2] + rnorm(n) # Y depends on Z, not on X  result_null <- bd_hsic_test(x, y, z,   n_permutations = 200,   seed = 1 ) result_null #>  #>    bd-HSIC Test #>  #> Statistic: 0.0022688  #> P-value:   0.4826  #> N:         150  #> Perms:     200  #> Kernel X:  rbf (bw = 1.062) #> Kernel Y:  rbf (bw = 1.553) #> ESS:       149.7

title: Causal Association Testing with bd-HSIC
text: key advantage bd-HSIC detecting non-linear effects linear methods (PDS, Double ML) completely miss:
code: set.seed(42) n <- 400 z <- matrix(rnorm(n * 2), n, 2) x <- z[, 1] + rnorm(n) y <- x^2 + z[, 2] + rnorm(n, sd = 0.5) # Quadratic causal effect  result_nl <- bd_hsic_test(x, y, z,   n_permutations = 200,   seed = 1 ) result_nl #>  #>    bd-HSIC Test #>  #> Statistic: 0.0206771  #> P-value:   0.0050  #> N:         200  #> Perms:     200  #> Kernel X:  rbf (bw = 1.299) #> Kernel Y:  rbf (bw = 2.029) #> ESS:       199.6

title: Calibration and concordance: kernR's validation layer
text: kernel Stein discrepancy needs score target – gradient log density – target may unnormalised reference sample required. multivariate-normal target score available closed form via gaussian_score(). verdict consistent target: statistic sits near zero p-value large. mis-specified sample – shifted mean – caught. target convenient closed-form score, supply log density let numeric_score() take gradient finite differences. additive normalising constant cancels, unnormalised log density enough. adapter lets ksd_test() consume externally supplied log-posterior evaluator: wrap evaluator numeric_score() test asks whether posterior draws calibrated posterior, dependency producer evaluator. default base kernel inverse multi-quadric, stays sensitive mis-specification higher dimensions Gaussian kernel can lose power; pass kernel = "rbf" Gaussian alternative.
code: set.seed(1)  # A sample that genuinely follows the standard normal x_ok <- matrix(rnorm(400), ncol = 2) ksd_test(x_ok, n_boot = 199, seed = 1) #>  #>    KSD Test #>  #> Statistic: -0.00225082  #> P-value:   0.6300  #> N:         200  #> Perms:     199  #> Kernel X:  imq #>  #> Goodness-of-fit verdict #>   Stein kernel: imq (beta = -0.5) #>   Bandwidth:    1.608 (median heuristic) #>   Bootstrap:    wild, B = 199 #>   Surprise:     0.667 bits #>   Verdict:      consistent with target x_bad <- x_ok + 0.7 ksd_test(x_bad, n_boot = 199, seed = 1) #>  #>    KSD Test #>  #> Statistic: 0.477917  #> P-value:   0.0050  #> N:         200  #> Perms:     199  #> Kernel X:  imq #>  #> Goodness-of-fit verdict #>   Stein kernel: imq (beta = -0.5) #>   Bandwidth:    1.608 (median heuristic) #>   Bootstrap:    wild, B = 199 #>   Surprise:     7.644 bits #>   Verdict:      REJECT (sample inconsistent with target) log_density <- function(z) -0.5 * rowSums(z^2)   # standard normal, unnormalised ksd_test(x_ok, score = numeric_score(log_density), n_boot = 199, seed = 1) #>  #>    KSD Test #>  #> Statistic: -0.00225082  #> P-value:   0.6300  #> N:         200  #> Perms:     199  #> Kernel X:  imq #>  #> Goodness-of-fit verdict #>   Stein kernel: imq (beta = -0.5) #>   Bandwidth:    1.608 (median heuristic) #>   Bootstrap:    wild, B = 199 #>   Surprise:     0.667 bits #>   Verdict:      consistent with target

title: Calibration and concordance: kernR's validation layer
text: calibration checks one sample density, concordance checks several samples one another. input list – one element per source – named list labels sources output. three sources mutually concordant, verdict concordant. test single yes--, though: returns full pairwise discrepancy matrix, one source departs rejection can read offending pair. pairwise matrix localises problem: engine_c row column carry large discrepancies, engine_a engine_b remain close. single joint-permutation null keeps overall p-value calibrated across pairs, one test one verdict, multiple-comparison sweep.
code: set.seed(2) draws <- list(   engine_a = matrix(rnorm(400), ncol = 2),   engine_b = matrix(rnorm(400), ncol = 2),   engine_c = matrix(rnorm(400), ncol = 2) ) concordance_test(draws, n_permutations = 199, seed = 1) #>  #>    Concordance Test #>  #> Statistic: 0.00131634  #> P-value:   0.3650  #> N:         600  #> Perms:     199  #> Kernel X:  rbf (bw = 1.682) #>  #> Concordance verdict #>   Sources:    3 (engine_a, engine_b, engine_c) #>   Verdict:    concordant #>   Pairwise MMD-squared: #>          engine_a engine_b  engine_c  #> engine_a    0     0.00165   0.000591  #> engine_b 0.00165     0      -0.000929 #> engine_c 0.000591 -0.000929    0 draws$engine_c <- draws$engine_c + 1   # engine_c now disagrees fit <- concordance_test(draws, n_permutations = 199, seed = 1) fit$pairwise #>             engine_a    engine_b  engine_c #> engine_a 0.000000000 0.001600717 0.1787739 #> engine_b 0.001600717 0.000000000 0.2067248 #> engine_c 0.178773878 0.206724754 0.0000000

title: Calibration and concordance: kernR's validation layer
text: Used together two tests express simple discipline. Concordance across independent sources corroborating evidence – agreement hard fake. Calibration target density absolute check agreed-upon answer also correct one. Divergence either test informative: concordance_test() localises source departs, ksd_test() identifies whether sample departs claimed target. pipeline whose ensembles pass earned trust one validated posterior mean alone.

title: Kernel-Based Downscaling: Vector and Distribution Inputs
text: kernR ships three downscaling primitives, covering distinct data-availability regimes: kernel_downscale() – vector-/ vector-. Conditional mean embedding (CME) regression: training observation single coarse-resolution vector; predict fine-resolution outputs new coarse inputs. Standard kernel ridge regression conditional-distribution form. Used “coarse” “fine” live kind object (e.g. one row per year). dist_regression() – bag-/ vector-. Distribution regression: training input bag points (sample distribution); predict scalar vector bag level. Used coarse covariate distribution rather single vector – e.g. paddock’s soil-core measurements, ensemble climate-model realisations. aggregate_downscale() – aggregate-likelihood inversion known aggregator. paired (coarse, fine) training data — aggregate observation y operator T Y = T(X) + eps. Gaussian-mixture prior latent X inverted closed form (linear-Gaussian T) importance sampling within prior component (non-linear T). Sejdinovic kernel-downsizing / aggregate-likelihood direction; companion proxymix from_aggregate_likelihood() Tier-2 stub. first two closed-form solutions; third closed form linear aggregators Monte-Carlo path non-linear. three use orders magnitude less data deep-learning downscalers carry interpretable bandwidth / regularisation / component-count knobs.

title: Kernel-Based Downscaling: Vector and Distribution Inputs
text: Predict held-coarse grid: lambda CV-selected via leave-one-; bandwidths follow median heuristic per kernel. can overridden.
code: library(kernR)  # Training: 8 years of coarse climate -> paddock-level yield/biomass n <- 80L coarse <- matrix(   c(stats::rnorm(n, mean = 18, sd = 2),    # mean monthly temperature     stats::rnorm(n, mean = 450, sd = 80)), # cumulative rainfall (mm)   ncol = 2L,   dimnames = list(NULL, c("temp", "rainfall")) ) truth <- function(z) {   cbind(     yield   = 0.02 * z[, "rainfall"] - 0.1 * (z[, "temp"] - 18)^2 +               stats::rnorm(nrow(z), sd = 0.5),     biomass = 0.03 * z[, "rainfall"] + 0.05 * z[, "temp"] +               stats::rnorm(nrow(z), sd = 0.7)   ) } fine <- truth(coarse) new_coarse <- matrix(   c(stats::rnorm(20L, 18, 2),     stats::rnorm(20L, 450, 80)),   ncol = 2L,   dimnames = list(NULL, c("temp", "rainfall")) ) fit <- kernel_downscale(coarse, fine, new_coarse) fit #>  #>   Kernel Downscaling (CME) #>  #> Training pairs:   80  #> Prediction points: 20  #> Output dims:      2  #> Kernel (coarse):  rbf (bw = 72.69) #> Lambda (ridge):   0.0003162 head(fit$prediction) #>           yield  biomass #> [1,] 10.1869683 16.11029 #> [2,]  7.5849960 11.98351 #> [3,]  9.0823386 15.11778 #> [4,]  7.9127329 13.75706 #> [5,]  0.8226318  9.93738 #> [6,]  6.3918668 10.91197

title: Kernel-Based Downscaling: Vector and Distribution Inputs
text: training prediction coarse-resolution shape: row one coarse vector. want point predictions fine resolution, full conditional distribution. Sample size tens--thousands range; closed-form ridge scales O(n^3) train-time solve predicts O(n_new * n_train). large training sets, plug nystrom_factor() outputs custom CME writing K_x = F F^T re-solving ridge factor space – see [fit_cme()] operator-level access.

title: Kernel-Based Downscaling: Vector and Distribution Inputs
text: simplest illustrative case: predict mean distribution distribution , given finite sample.
code: M <- 60L mu_train <- stats::runif(M, -3, 3) bags_train <- lapply(mu_train, function(m) {   matrix(stats::rnorm(40L, mean = m, sd = 1), ncol = 1L) })  fit_dr <- dist_regression(bags_train, y = mu_train, outer = "linear") fit_dr #>  #>   Kernel Distribution Regression #>  #> Training bags:     60  #> Bag sizes:        40-40 (median 40) #> Point dim:         1  #> Output dim:        1  #> Inner kernel:      rbf (bw =  2.01) #> Outer kernel:      linear #> Ridge lambda:      0.0001 # Predict on fresh bags M_new <- 10L mu_new <- stats::runif(M_new, -3, 3) bags_new <- lapply(mu_new, function(m) {   matrix(stats::rnorm(40L, mean = m, sd = 1), ncol = 1L) }) pred <- predict(fit_dr, bags_new) data.frame(   truth      = round(mu_new, 3L),   prediction = round(pred,    3L),   abs_error  = round(abs(pred - mu_new), 3L) ) #>     truth prediction abs_error #> 1  -2.258     -2.039     0.219 #> 2   0.256      0.323     0.067 #> 3  -0.717     -0.658     0.059 #> 4  -0.159     -0.066     0.094 #> 5   2.827      2.855     0.028 #> 6  -2.241     -2.323     0.082 #> 7   0.338      0.684     0.346 #> 8  -1.333     -1.112     0.221 #> 9   0.221      0.325     0.104 #> 10  1.103      1.140     0.036

code: y_mat <- cbind(mean = mu_train, mean_sq = mu_train^2) fit_mv <- dist_regression(bags_train, y = y_mat, outer = "linear") predict(fit_mv, bags_new[1:3]) #>            mean   mean_sq #> [1,] -2.0388518 4.4290140 #> [2,]  0.3229703 0.2034361 #> [3,] -0.6579623 0.6030948

title: Kernel-Based Downscaling: Vector and Distribution Inputs
text: outer = "linear" – inner-product empirical embeddings. appropriate target well captured first moment bag (e.g. predicting bag mean). outer = "rbf" – Gaussian embedding-space distance. Picks higher-order distributional features. Use target depends bag mean (variance, skew, tails). Bandwidth follows median heuristic embedding-space pairwise distances.
code: # Variance-targeting example: zero-mean bags with varying SDs s_train <- stats::runif(M, 0.5, 2.5) bags_var <- lapply(s_train, function(s) {   matrix(stats::rnorm(60L, mean = 0, sd = s), ncol = 1L) })  # Linear outer: insensitive to bag SD (means are all near zero) fit_lin <- dist_regression(bags_var, y = s_train, outer = "linear") fit_lin #>  #>   Kernel Distribution Regression #>  #> Training bags:     60  #> Bag sizes:        60-60 (median 60) #> Point dim:         1  #> Output dim:        1  #> Inner kernel:      rbf (bw = 1.518) #> Outer kernel:      linear #> Ridge lambda:      3.162e-05  # RBF outer: embedding-space distance differs even with equal means fit_rbf <- dist_regression(bags_var, y = s_train, outer = "rbf") fit_rbf #>  #>   Kernel Distribution Regression #>  #> Training bags:     60  #> Bag sizes:        60-60 (median 60) #> Point dim:         1  #> Output dim:        1  #> Inner kernel:      rbf (bw = 1.582) #> Outer kernel:      rbf (bw = 0.1897) #> Ridge lambda:      0.001

title: Kernel-Based Downscaling: Vector and Distribution Inputs
text: Bag sizes may differ across design (e.g. paddocks different numbers soil cores). double-sum embedding handles naturally via mean() inner Gram:
code: mu_v <- stats::runif(20L, -2, 2) bags_v <- lapply(mu_v, function(m) {   n_i <- sample(20:80, 1L)   matrix(stats::rnorm(n_i, mean = m), ncol = 1L) }) fit_v <- dist_regression(bags_v, y = mu_v, outer = "linear") range(vapply(fit_v$bags_train, nrow, integer(1L))) #> [1] 23 80

title: Kernel-Based Downscaling: Vector and Distribution Inputs
text: training row collection measurements rather single vector (paddock soil cores, ensemble realisations, spectral samples, repeated experimental measurements). target property underlying distribution, single point. Sample size bags: 10s-100s points per bag; number bags 10s-low 1000s. Cost O(M^2 \cdot \bar{m}^2) inner Gram M bag count, \bar{m} mean bag size.

title: Kernel-Based Downscaling: Vector and Distribution Inputs
text: Often downscaling problem looks nothing like supervised regression: paired (coarse, fine) data available. Instead, observation y = T(x) + eps T known operator (spatial average, temporal average, satellite footprint convolution, non-linear sensor model), eps observation noise, parametric prior fine-scale latent x available — example, fitted via proxymix::fit_proxymix() historical fine-scale data, specified directly. job recover posterior p(x | y). aggregate_downscale() dispatches aggregator’s class: Linear matrix (y = x + eps): closed-form per-component Kalman update plus reweighted mixture weights. Cost O(N (dim_x^3 + dim_y^3)). Function T(x) (non-linear): importance sampling within prior component (n_samples_per_component draws, default 200). Per-component effective sample size reported ESS-floor warning fires collapses. Cost O(N M (dim_x + dim_y)) per call. Linear example — spatial averaging two adjacent paddocks two-cluster prior: posterior mixture weights shift toward cluster consistent observation (, (2, 2) cluster). per-component posterior exact (Kalman update) cross-component law-- total-covariance honest. Non-linear example — sinusoidal aggregator (sensor non-linearity): per-component ESS reliability gate — collapses, function warns posterior treated exploratory. Draw posterior samples downstream uncertainty propagation:

title: DR-DATE for APSIM Scenario Counterfactuals
text: use synthetic linear-Gaussian forward model place APSIM vignette runs without APSIM install. Replace forward PESTO::apsim_callback(...) drive real APSIM ensemble. Calibrate PESTO’s -process IES driver: Now forward-simulate posterior two scenarios. intervention deterministic shift outputs — real ag application e.g. stubble-retention management rule changes APSIM’s predicted yield trajectory. Wrap scenario manifest. two manifests share parameter posterior — ’s canonical structure. production build manifests via as_manifest() two separate IES runs scenarios genuinely produced different calibration data. “intervention shift forward outputs?” question, -posterior construction right one.
code: npar  <- 2L nobs  <- 4L nreal <- 60L sigma <- 0.05  G <- matrix(rnorm(nobs * npar), nobs, npar) y_obs <- as.numeric(G %*% c(1.0, -0.5)) + rnorm(nobs, sd = sigma) names(y_obs) <- paste0("o", seq_len(nobs))  prior <- matrix(rnorm(nreal * npar), nreal, npar,                 dimnames = list(NULL, c("p1", "p2"))) forward <- function(theta) theta %*% t(G) fit <- pesto_ies_callback(   forward_model  = forward,   prior_ensemble = prior,   obs            = y_obs,   obs_sd         = sigma,   noptmax        = 3L,   verbose        = FALSE ) par_post   <- as.matrix(fit$par_ensemble[, c("p1", "p2"), with = FALSE]) out_base   <- par_post %*% t(G) out_intv   <- out_base + 0.6                       # the intervention colnames(out_base) <- colnames(out_intv) <- names(y_obs) real_names <- fit$par_ensemble$real_name  m_baseline <- pesto_ensemble_manifest(   run_id          = "wagga_baseline_2026",   params          = data.frame(real_name = real_names, par_post,                                check.names = FALSE),   outputs         = data.frame(real_name = real_names, out_base,                                check.names = FALSE),   weights         = setNames(rep(1 / sigma, nobs), names(y_obs)),   obs_target      = setNames(y_obs, names(y_obs)),   data_hash       = "sha256:vignette_baseline",   pesto_version   = as.character(packageVersion("PESTO")),   timestamp       = Sys.time(),   method          = "ies_callback",   noptmax         = 3L,   lambda_schedule = 1 )  m_intervention <- pesto_ensemble_manifest(   run_id          = "wagga_stubble_2026",   params          = data.frame(real_name = real_names, par_post,                                check.names = FALSE),     # SAME params   outputs         = data.frame(real_name = real_names, out_intv,                                check.names = FALSE),   weights         = setNames(rep(1 / sigma, nobs), names(y_obs)),   obs_target      = setNames(y_obs, names(y_obs)),   data_hash       = "sha256:vignette_intervention",   pesto_version   = as.character(packageVersion("PESTO")),   timestamp       = Sys.time(),   method          = "ies_callback",   noptmax         = 3L,   lambda_schedule = 1 ) m_baseline #> <pesto_ensemble_manifest> schema 1.0.0 #>   run_id        : wagga_baseline_2026 #>   method        : ies_callback  (noptmax=3) #>   ensemble      : 60 realisations x 2 parameters | 4 observations #>   failure rate  : 0.00% #>   pesto version : 0.5.0  apsim: NA #>   timestamp     : 2026-06-03T00:22:06+0000 #>   data hash     : sha256:vignette_baseline

title: Distributional Treatment Effect Tests (DR-DATE / DR-DETT)
text: Standard causal inference methods (Double ML, TMLE) test whether treatment shifts mean outcome. many real treatment effects distributional – change variance, shape, modality without necessarily changing mean. DR-DATE DR-DETT (Fawkes, Hu, Evans & Sejdinovic, 2024) test distributional difference Y(1) Y(0), using doubly robust kernel embeddings.

title: Distributional Treatment Effect Tests (DR-DATE / DR-DETT)
text: DR-DATE (Distributional Average Treatment Effect): Tests whether P(Y(1)) = P(Y(0)) entire population. DR-DETT (Distributional Effect Treated): Tests whether P(Y(1)|T=1) = P(Y(0)|T=1), focusing treated subgroup. Requires one-sided overlap. Double robustness: Consistent either propensity model outcome model correctly specified.

what: Example: Mean Shift (Detectable by All Methods)
title: Distributional Treatment Effect Tests (DR-DATE / DR-DETT)
code: library(kernR) set.seed(42)  n <- 300 x <- matrix(rnorm(n * 2), n, 2) logit_p <- 0.3 * x[, 1] - 0.2 * x[, 2] t <- rbinom(n, 1, plogis(logit_p)) y <- t * 1.0 + 0.5 * x[, 1] + rnorm(n, sd = 0.5) # Mean shift of 1.0  result <- dr_date_test(y, t, x,   n_permutations = 200,   seed = 1 ) result #>  #>    DR-DATE Test #>  #> Statistic: 0.37054  #> P-value:   0.0050  #> N:         300  #> Perms:     200  #> Kernel Y:  rbf (bw = 0.8972) #> ESS:       139.7

title: Distributional Treatment Effect Tests (DR-DATE / DR-DETT)
text: DR-DATE shines. treatment changes variance outcome mean – DML TMLE zero power .
code: set.seed(42) n <- 400 x <- matrix(rnorm(n * 2), n, 2) t <- rbinom(n, 1, plogis(0.3 * x[, 1]))  # Treatment doubles the variance but does NOT shift the mean y <- (1 - t) * rnorm(n, sd = 1) + t * rnorm(n, sd = 2.5) + 0.5 * x[, 1]  cat("Mean difference:", mean(y[t == 1]) - mean(y[t == 0]), "\n") #> Mean difference: 0.5228389 cat("SD treated:", sd(y[t == 1]), "  SD control:", sd(y[t == 0]), "\n") #> SD treated: 2.658814   SD control: 1.076454  result_var <- dr_date_test(y, t, x,   n_permutations = 200,   outcome_model = "zero",   seed = 1 ) result_var #>  #>    DR-DATE Test #>  #> Statistic: 0.160419  #> P-value:   0.0050  #> N:         400  #> Perms:     200  #> Kernel Y:  rbf (bw = 1.684) #> ESS:       176.9

what: Why a hierarchical version of bd-HSIC?
title: Hierarchical bd-HSIC for Multi-Site Designs
text: Agricultural trials almost always carry design clustering: paddocks within sites, sites within regions, plots within farms, seasons within years. Treat data ..d. permutation null breaks: shuffling treatment labels across clusters, null distribution absorbs cluster-level variation causal signal, inflating Type error. original bd-HSIC test (Hu, Sejdinovic & Evans, 2024) handles clustering propensity-score similarity. works design natural hierarchy. design tells observations exchangeable – site, paddock, season – use design clusters directly. bd_hsic_test() accepts optional cluster_id argument exactly case. Supplying activates within-cluster permutation: indices y reshuffled within cluster, preserving cluster-level effects null.

title: Hierarchical bd-HSIC for Multi-Site Designs
code: library(kernR)  # 8 sites x 30 plots/site = 240 observations. # Each site has its own random effect on yield. n_sites <- 8L n_per   <- 30L n       <- n_sites * n_per site    <- factor(paste0("site", rep(seq_len(n_sites), each = n_per))) site_id <- as.integer(site)  set.seed(101L) site_effect <- stats::rnorm(n_sites, sd = 1.0)[site_id]  # Confounders: rainfall, soil_n z <- cbind(   rainfall = stats::rnorm(n),   soil_n   = stats::rnorm(n) ) # Management treatment (e.g. residue retention intensity) x <- 0.4 * z[, "rainfall"] + 0.3 * site_effect + stats::rnorm(n) # Yield: causal effect of management + confounders + site random effect y <- 0.6 * x + 0.5 * z[, "soil_n"] + site_effect + stats::rnorm(n, sd = 0.5)

title: Hierarchical bd-HSIC for Multi-Site Designs
text: data, two permutation schemes: presence true causal effect, schemes typically reject; null, naive scheme prone false positives.
code: fit_naive <- bd_hsic_test(   x, y, z,   permutation    = "naive",   n_permutations = 199L,   seed           = 1L )  fit_hier <- bd_hsic_test(   x, y, z,   cluster_id     = site,        # activates within_cluster by default   n_permutations = 199L,   seed           = 1L )  data.frame(   scheme  = c(fit_naive$permutation_scheme, fit_hier$permutation_scheme),   stat    = c(fit_naive$statistic,   fit_hier$statistic),   p_value = c(fit_naive$p_value,     fit_hier$p_value),   ess     = c(fit_naive$ess,         fit_hier$ess),   row.names = NULL ) #>           scheme       stat p_value      ess #> 1          naive 0.02198144   0.005 119.9986 #> 2 within_cluster 0.02198144   0.005 119.9986

title: Hierarchical bd-HSIC for Multi-Site Designs
text: illustrate hierarchical scheme matters, null case: cluster effects exist, x causal effect y. null, hierarchical scheme reject (p > 0.05); naive scheme data likely .
code: set.seed(202L) n_sites <- 8L; n_per <- 30L; n <- n_sites * n_per site <- factor(paste0("s", rep(seq_len(n_sites), each = n_per))) site_id <- as.integer(site) site_effect <- stats::rnorm(n_sites, sd = 1.0)[site_id] z <- cbind(stats::rnorm(n), stats::rnorm(n)) x <- 0.4 * z[, 1L] + 0.3 * site_effect + stats::rnorm(n) y <- 0.5 * z[, 2L] + site_effect + stats::rnorm(n, sd = 0.5)  # no x  fit_null <- bd_hsic_test(   x, y, z,   cluster_id     = site,   n_permutations = 199L,   seed           = 202L ) fit_null$p_value #> [1] 0.925

title: Hierarchical bd-HSIC for Multi-Site Designs
text: Cluster size. Within-cluster permutation needs >= 2 test observations per cluster (test split = 1 - split_ratio n). Smaller clusters reported NA per_cluster_statistic contribute identity permutation. Many small clusters. clusters small (e.g. paddocks 3-5 plots), within-cluster permutation limited power; consider pooling coarser level (site instead paddock). Imbalanced design. Per-cluster statistics reported raw scale, weighted cluster size; combine externally need size-weighted summary.

title: Hierarchical and Nested Data
text: Many real-world datasets nested structure: Patients within hospitals Students within schools Plots within farms Repeated measures within subjects Standard kernel tests assume independent observations. observations clustered, within-cluster correlation inflates type error. hierarchical_test() accounts decomposing test statistic permuting within clusters.

title: Hierarchical and Nested Data
text: Imagine randomised fertiliser trial across 20 farms, 30 plots. test detects treatment effect correctly accounting farm-level clustering.
code: library(kernR) set.seed(42)  n_farms <- 20 n_plots <- 30 n <- n_farms * n_plots farm_id <- rep(1:n_farms, each = n_plots)  # Farm-level random effects farm_effect <- rnorm(n_farms, sd = 2)[farm_id] soil <- matrix(rnorm(n * 2), n, 2)  # Treatment assignment (partially confounded by soil) treatment <- rbinom(n, 1, plogis(0.3 * soil[, 1]))  # Yield: treatment has a real effect + farm random effect yield <- 0.8 * treatment + farm_effect + 0.5 * soil[, 1] + rnorm(n)  result <- hierarchical_test(   y = yield,   treatment = treatment,   covariates = soil,   cluster_id = farm_id,   method = "dr-date",   n_permutations = 100,   weight_method = "icc",   seed = 1 ) result #>  #>    Hierarchical-DRDATE Test #>  #> Statistic: 0.00874629  #> P-value:   0.0099  #> N:         600  #> Perms:     100  #> Kernel Y:  rbf (bw = 2.642)

title: Pre-IES Identifiability Screening with HSIC
text: Iterative Ensemble-Smoother (IES) calibration mechanistic agricultural model APSIM expensive: ensemble member full simulator run. Spending realisations parameters detectable effect outputs interest wasted budget. pre-IES screen answers sharper question variance-based Sobol analysis: parameter influence distribution output ? HSIC – Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion – detects linear non-linear, mean-shift tail-shift effects, including Sobol misses variance preserved. workflow three lines: Build Latin-hypercube design across plausible parameter ranges. Run simulator design. Pass design + outputs hsic_identifiability().

title: Pre-IES Identifiability Screening with HSIC
text: Real APSIM workflows heavy, vignette uses small simulator stub four parameters – two genuinely identifiable, one weakly nonlinear, one inert – two outputs (yield biomass).
code: library(kernR)  stub_apsim <- function(theta) {   # theta: n x 4 matrix with columns slope, curvature, weak, inert   yield   <- 1.5 * theta[, "slope"] +              0.3 * stats::rnorm(nrow(theta), sd = 0.1)   biomass <- theta[, "curvature"]^2 +              0.4 * theta[, "weak"] +              stats::rnorm(nrow(theta), sd = 0.1)   cbind(yield = yield, biomass = biomass) }

title: Pre-IES Identifiability Screening with HSIC
text: Design size. n comfortably exceed number parameters; recommend n >= 10p starting point ag-systems-scale designs. Permutations. 199-499 permutations usually sufficient; minimum achievable p-value n_permutations 1 / (n_permutations + 1), n_permutations >= 199 floor testing alpha = 0.05. Multiple testing. many parameters several outputs, unadjusted p-values produce spurious “identifiable” flags. default Benjamini-Hochberg adjustment controls false discovery rate across p x q grid. Cost. Kernel matrices reused across grid, cost scales O((p + q) n^2) kernel construction plus O(p q B n^2) permutation null, B = n_permutations.

H., Song, L., Schölkopf, B., & Smola, . J. (2008). kernel statistical test independence. NeurIPS, 20. Da Veiga, S. (2015). Global sensitivity analysis dependence measures. title: Scaling HSIC with Nystrom and Random Fourier Features
text: Exact kernel methods carry O(n^2) storage cost (Gram matrix) O(n^2) O(n^3) compute per operation. ag-systems ensembles low thousands members, wall hits around n = 5000: 5000 x 5000 double-precision Gram matrix 200 MB, permutation HSIC iterates B times. Two well-known low-rank approximations kernel matrix K (n x n) drop costs O(n m) rank m << n: Nystrom (Williams & Seeger, 2001) – sample m landmark observations, compute Gram W (m x m) cross-Gram C (n x m), K \approx C W^{-1} C^\top = F F^\top F = C \cdot \mathrm{chol}(W)^{-1}. Works kernel. Random Fourier Features (Rahimi & Recht, 2007) – shift-invariant kernels (currently RBF kernR), draw \omega_k \sim N(0, \sigma^{-2} ) b_k \sim U[0, 2\pi]; feature map \phi(x) = \sqrt{2/D} \cos(\omega^\top x + b) gives K \approx \Phi \Phi^\top D features. Data-independent (random projection applies point cloud bandwidth). hsic_test_nystrom() wires drop-accelerated HSIC test.

title: Scaling HSIC with Nystrom and Random Fourier Features
text: approximations recover exact kernel rank approaches n (Nystrom) D grows (RFF). produce small relative Frobenius error size.
code: library(kernR)  n <- 80L x <- matrix(stats::rnorm(n * 2L), n, 2L) k <- kernel_spec("rbf", bandwidth = 1.0) K_full <- kernel_matrix(x, kernel = k)  # Nystrom at near-full rank ny <- nystrom_factor(x, kernel = k, m = n - 1L, seed = 1L) K_ny <- tcrossprod(ny$F)  # RFF at a moderate D rf <- rff_features(x, kernel = k, D = 1500L, seed = 1L) K_rf <- tcrossprod(rf$F)  rel_err <- function(K_hat) {   sqrt(sum((K_full - K_hat)^2)) / sqrt(sum(K_full^2)) } data.frame(   method = c("nystrom", "rff"),   rank   = c(ncol(ny$F), ncol(rf$F)),   rel_err = c(rel_err(K_ny), rel_err(K_rf)) ) #>    method rank      rel_err #> 1 nystrom   79 1.849946e-07 #> 2     rff 1500 4.728235e-02

title: Scaling HSIC with Nystrom and Random Fourier Features
text: larger n, gap widens: verdict (reject vs accept) agrees across exact approximate tests moderate m; approximation speed, detecting different signal.
code: benchmark_hsic <- function(n, m, B = 49L, seed = 1L) {   set.seed(seed)   xx <- stats::rnorm(n)   y  <- xx + stats::rnorm(n, sd = 0.5)    t_exact <- system.time(     res_exact <- hsic_test(xx, y, n_permutations = B, seed = seed)   )["elapsed"]   t_nystrom <- system.time(     res_nystrom <- hsic_test_nystrom(xx, y, m = m,                                      n_permutations = B, seed = seed)   )["elapsed"]   t_rff <- system.time(     res_rff <- hsic_test_nystrom(xx, y, method = "rff", m = m,                                  n_permutations = B, seed = seed)   )["elapsed"]   data.frame(     n         = n,     m         = m,     method    = c("hsic_test (exact)", "nystrom", "rff"),     elapsed_s = round(c(t_exact, t_nystrom, t_rff), 3),     p_value   = round(c(res_exact$p_value, res_nystrom$p_value,                         res_rff$p_value), 3)   ) }  benchmark_hsic(n = 500L,  m = 60L) #>     n  m            method elapsed_s p_value #> 1 500 60 hsic_test (exact)     0.094    0.02 #> 2 500 60           nystrom     0.020    0.02 #> 3 500 60               rff     0.017    0.02 benchmark_hsic(n = 1500L, m = 100L) #>      n   m            method elapsed_s p_value #> 1 1500 100 hsic_test (exact)     1.242    0.02 #> 2 1500 100           nystrom     0.135    0.02 #> 3 1500 100               rff     0.133    0.02

title: Scaling HSIC with Nystrom and Random Fourier Features
text: Bias vs unbiased estimator. exact hsic_test() uses Gretton-2008 unbiased HSIC. Nystrom/RFF version uses biased estimator (1/n^2) tr(H K_x H K_y), form factors cleanly low-rank approximations. bias O(1/n) – negligible large-n regime approximations useful. Choosing m Nystrom. Rule thumb: m = ceiling(2 sqrt(n)) defensible starting point. Diagnose via Frobenius reconstruction error small subsample precision matters. Choosing D RFF. RFF variance scales O(1/D); bias zero expectation. Larger D always better accuracy costs memory; D = 200-500 usually adequate verdict-style tests. Reproducibility. factorisations randomised; pass seed deterministic output.

title: Posterior-Predictive Check with MMD
text: Ensemble size. Distributional power set ensemble size M held-sample size n_obs. ag-scale problems M low hundreds usually sufficient; n_obs >= 20 practical floor. Permutations. B = 299 minimum achievable p-value 1 / 300 ~ 0.0033; correspondingly maximum surprise log2(300) ~ 8.23 bits. Increase B working small-p regime. Interpreting surprise. Surprise Shannon information permutation p-value: 0 bits p = 1, 4.32 bits p = 0.05, capped log2(B + 1). strict Bayesian-surprise KL divergence; use intuitive scalar verdict, posterior probability. Multi-dimensional outputs. mmd_ppc() handles d > 1 natively via kernel choice; default RBF median-heuristic bandwidth computed pooled posterior + observed sample.

title: Posterior-Predictive Check with MMD
text: PESTO 0.3.0 ships native ensemble emitter — PESTO::pesto_ensemble_manifest S7 class — mmd_ppc() consumes via dedicated method. legacy lightweight pesto_ensemble S3 constructor unchanged remains supported. outputs = argument focuses check specific observation columns (parallel dr_date_scenario()’s convention).
code: library(PESTO)  # Build a tiny synthetic manifest (in real workflows: come from # pesto_ies_callback() + as_manifest()). nreal <- 80L; nobs <- 3L post   <- matrix(rnorm(nreal * nobs), nreal, nobs) colnames(post) <- paste0("o", seq_len(nobs)) m <- pesto_ensemble_manifest(   run_id          = "vignette_demo",   params          = data.frame(real_name = paste0("r", seq_len(nreal)),                                 p1 = rnorm(nreal),                                 p2 = rnorm(nreal),                                 check.names = FALSE),   outputs         = data.frame(real_name = paste0("r", seq_len(nreal)),                                 post, check.names = FALSE),   weights         = setNames(rep(1, nobs), colnames(post)),   obs_target      = setNames(rnorm(nobs),  colnames(post)),   data_hash       = "sha256:vignette_demo",   pesto_version   = as.character(packageVersion("PESTO")),   timestamp       = Sys.time(),   method          = "ies_callback",   noptmax         = 1L,   lambda_schedule = 1 )  # Out-of-sample PPC: supply held-out observations explicitly. The # manifest's `obs_target` slot is a single nobs-dim point (the data # the posterior was fit to) and is not a valid two-sample comparator # on its own. held_out <- matrix(rnorm(20L * nobs), 20L, nobs) colnames(held_out) <- colnames(post) mmd_ppc(m, observed = held_out, n_permutations = 199L, seed = 1L) #>  #>    MMD PPC Test #>  #> Statistic: -0.00916666  #> P-value:   0.7250  #> N:         100  #> Perms:     199  #> Kernel X:  rbf (bw =  2.25) #>  #> PPC verdict #>   Posterior:  80 draws #>   Observed:   20 obs #>   Surprise:   0.464 bits #>   Verdict:    consistent with observations #>   Metadata:   run_id, pesto_version, method, outputs_used, fidelity

title: Density-Ratio Backends and the proxymix Binding

title: Density-Ratio Backends and the proxymix Binding
text: toy confounded design: z 2-D Gaussian confounder; x linear-Gaussian function z; y carries real causal effect x plus confounded path z. backend produces density_ratio_fit object exposing ESS weight vector. tabulate side--side. Density-ratio diagnostics across backends.
code: suppressPackageStartupMessages(library(kernR)) set.seed(2026L) n <- 200L z <- matrix(rnorm(n * 2L), n, 2L) x <- z[, 1L] + rnorm(n, sd = 0.5) y <- 0.7 * x + z[, 2L] + rnorm(n, sd = 0.4) dr_logistic <- estimate_density_ratio(x, z, method = "logistic", seed = 1L) dr_ranger <- if (requireNamespace("ranger", quietly = TRUE)) {   estimate_density_ratio(x, z, method = "ranger", seed = 1L) } else NULL dr_xgb <- if (requireNamespace("xgboost", quietly = TRUE)) {   estimate_density_ratio(x, z, method = "xgboost", seed = 1L) } else NULL dr_proxymix <- estimate_density_ratio(   x, z,   method = "proxymix",   proxymix_components = 2L,   seed = 1L )

The weighted #> test statistic is dominated by a small number of high-weight observations; the #> resulting p-value is not a reliable verdict. Increase n, switch density_ratio #> backend, or tighten the design.

title: Density-Ratio Backends and the proxymix Binding
text: classifier-based backends (logistic, ranger, xgboost) default reason: tolerate misspecified densities, scale high-dimensional z, well-understood calibration. Reach method = "proxymix" : joint density plausibly multimodal (multi-regime climate, paddock × variety designs distinct production zones, animal cohorts separable subpopulations) — 2-component GMM represents cleanly classifier smear across modes; need parametric density-ratio whose components can inspect, hand downstream Bayesian step (via proxymix::gmm_target_from_posterior()), use seed KLD-EM refinement target can evaluate sample ; classifier calibration unreliable cohort hand (small n, sharp class imbalance joint-vs-marginal split, pathological feature scaling). proxymix package GRDC-firewalled (MIT, GRDC IP flows ) ships full Gaussian-mixture proxy API independently kernR. kernR consumes soft dependency via requireNamespace() — binding one-way rebuildable local proxymix_*.tar.gz source.

title: Getting Started with kernR
text: kernR provides kernel-based statistical tests causal inference distributional comparison. implements: - HSIC test: independence testing via Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion - MMD test: two-sample testing via Maximum Mean Discrepancy - bd-HSIC test: causal association testing backdoor adjustment - DR-DATE / DR-DETT: doubly robust distributional treatment effect tests vignette covers basics: kernels, MMD, HSIC.

title: Getting Started with kernR

title: Getting Started with kernR
text: MMD test asks: two samples come distribution? p-value large — evidence different distributions. Now mean shift: small p-value correctly detects distributional difference.
code: set.seed(123)  # Two samples from the same distribution x <- matrix(rnorm(200), 100, 2) y <- matrix(rnorm(200), 100, 2)  result <- mmd_test(x, y, seed = 1) result #>  #>    MMD Test #>  #> Statistic: -0.004145  #> P-value:   0.7465  #> N:         200  #> Perms:     500  #> Kernel X:  rbf (bw =  1.61) y_shifted <- matrix(rnorm(200, mean = 0.5), 100, 2) result <- mmd_test(x, y_shifted, seed = 1) result #>  #>    MMD Test #>  #> Statistic: 0.0789288  #> P-value:   0.0020  #> N:         200  #> Perms:     500  #> Kernel X:  rbf (bw = 1.686)

title: Getting Started with kernR
text: HSIC tests whether two variables independent — including non-linear dependencies correlation miss. HSIC successfully detects quadratic relationship even though Pearson correlation near zero.
code: set.seed(456) n <- 300 x <- rnorm(n)  # Non-linear dependence: Y = X^2 + noise # Note: cor(x, y) is approximately 0 (no linear correlation) y <- x^2 + rnorm(n, sd = 0.3) cat("Pearson correlation:", round(cor(x, y), 3), "\n") #> Pearson correlation: 0.022  # HSIC detects the non-linear dependence result <- hsic_test(x, y, seed = 1) result #>  #>    HSIC Test #>  #> Statistic: 0.0258008  #> P-value:   0.0020  #> N:         300  #> Perms:     500  #> Kernel X:  rbf (bw = 0.9644) #> Kernel Y:  rbf (bw = 0.8461)

what: What HSIC-Sensitivity catches that Sobol misses
title: HSIC-Based Distributional Sensitivity
text: Variance-based Sobol indices workhorse global sensitivity analysis: decompose output variance contributions input. one structural blind spot – distributional effects leave variance unchanged. parameter flips output skewness, fattens tails, shifts quantile without moving variance invisible Sobol. HSIC-Sensitivity Index (Da Veiga, 2015) replaces variance kernel-based dependence measure (Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion). normalised first-order index SjHSIC=HSIC(Xj,Y)HSIC(Xj,Xj)⋅HSIC(Y,Y)∈[0,1]. S_j^{\mathrm{HSIC}}   = \frac{\mathrm{HSIC}(X_j, Y)}          {\sqrt{\mathrm{HSIC}(X_j, X_j) \cdot \mathrm{HSIC}(Y, Y)}}   \[0, 1]. bounded like Sobol index, comparable magnitude across parameters, detects mean-preserving distributional effects.

what: A sensitivity scan on a stubbed APSIM-like simulator
title: HSIC-Based Distributional Sensitivity
code: library(kernR)  stub_apsim <- function(theta) {   # theta: n x 4 matrix   # `mean_effect` shifts the mean (Sobol catches this)   # `var_effect`  shifts the variance only -- mean-preserving   # `tail_effect` injects a heavy-tailed component (visible to HSIC)   # `inert`       has no effect   n <- nrow(theta)   yield <- 1.5 * theta[, "mean_effect"] +            stats::rnorm(n, sd = exp(theta[, "var_effect"])) +            theta[, "tail_effect"] *              stats::rt(n, df = 3) * 0.4 +            stats::rnorm(n, sd = 0.05)   matrix(yield, ncol = 1L, dimnames = list(NULL, "yield")) }  bounds <- rbind(   mean_effect = c(0.0, 1.0),   var_effect  = c(-0.5, 0.5),   tail_effect = c(0.0, 1.0),   inert       = c(0.0, 1.0) ) design <- lhs_design(n = 200L, bounds = bounds, seed = 11L) y      <- stub_apsim(design)

title: HSIC-Based Distributional Sensitivity
text: print method ranks parameters first-order HSIC-Sensitivity Index. Expectations design: mean_effect – large index, small p-value (Sobol also catch ). var_effect – non-negligible index despite preserving conditional mean (Sobol misses ). tail_effect – detectable distributional contribution. inert – index near zero, p-value significant.
code: fit <- hsic_sensitivity(   theta          = design,   y              = y,   n_permutations = 199L,   seed           = 11L ) fit #>  #>   HSIC-Sensitivity Indices #>  #> Parameters:   4  #> Outputs:      1  #> N:            200  #> Permutations: 199  #> P-adjust:     BH  #> Total-order: no #>  #> Per-parameter ranking (descending S, max across outputs): #>   S = first-order index #>  #>    parameter S_first_max min_p_first #>  mean_effect       0.156      0.0200 #>   var_effect       0.020      0.1400 #>        inert       0.013      0.4000 #>  tail_effect       0.009      0.5250

title: HSIC-Based Distributional Sensitivity
text: first-order index S_j measures direct contribution X_j Y. misses contributions X_j interactions parameters. total-order index, Da Veiga’s complement formulation, TjHSIC=1−HSIC(X∼j,Y)HSIC(X∼j,X∼j)⋅HSIC(Y,Y) T_j^{\mathrm{HSIC}}   = 1 - \frac{\mathrm{HSIC}(X_{\sim j}, Y)}               {\sqrt{\mathrm{HSIC}(X_{\sim j}, X_{\sim j}) \cdot                      \mathrm{HSIC}(Y, Y)}} captures total – direct plus -order interactions – contribution X_j. construction: Purely additive model Y = sum_j f_j(X_j): T_j = S_j. Pure interaction Y = X_1 X_2: S_j may near zero (marginal signal averages ) T_j strong. difference T_j - S_j quantifies interactive component.

title: HSIC-Based Distributional Sensitivity
text: x1 x2 small first-order indices (marginal expectation X1 X2 zero) large total-order indices. interaction = T - S column makes gap explicit.
code: set.seed(42L) n <- 400L theta_int <- matrix(stats::runif(n * 2L, min = -1, max = 1), n, 2L,                     dimnames = list(NULL, c("x1", "x2"))) y_int <- theta_int[, "x1"] * theta_int[, "x2"] +          stats::rnorm(n, sd = 0.05)  fit_int <- hsic_sensitivity(   theta          = theta_int,   y              = y_int,   total_order    = TRUE,   p_value        = FALSE,    # total-order p-values not computed (see Notes)   n_permutations = 199L,   seed           = 42L ) fit_int #>  #>   HSIC-Sensitivity Indices #>  #> Parameters:   2  #> Outputs:      1  #> N:            400  #> Permutations: skipped (p_value = FALSE) #> Total-order: yes #>  #> Per-parameter ranking (descending S, max across outputs): #>   S = first-order index   T = total-order index   interaction = T - S #>  #>  parameter S_first_max T_total_max interaction min_p_first #>         x1       0.100       0.906       0.806          -- #>         x2       0.094       0.900       0.806          --

text: Cost. Total-order construction O(p n^2) kernel matrices X_{~j} plus O(p q n^2) HSIC, similar scale first-order. ag-scale designs (n hundreds-low thousands) practical. Pick-Freeze bootstrap multiplies n_bootstrap (default 200) — budget accordingly. title: Kernel-Based Causal Distributional Testing
code: library(kernR)  # Detect non-linear dependence (HSIC) set.seed(42) x <- rnorm(200) y <- x^2 + rnorm(200, sd = 0.3)  # quadratic relationship cor.test(x, y)$p.value            # Pearson: p = 0.85 (misses it!) hsic_test(x, y, seed = 1)        # HSIC:    p = 0.002 (detects it)  # Test for distributional treatment effect (DR-DATE) n <- 300 covariates <- matrix(rnorm(n * 2), n, 2) treatment  <- rbinom(n, 1, plogis(0.3 * covariates[, 1])) outcome    <- treatment * rnorm(n, sd = 2) +       # treatment changes variance               (1 - treatment) * rnorm(n, sd = 1) +  # but NOT the mean               covariates[, 1]  t.test(outcome[treatment == 1], outcome[treatment == 0])  # p = 0.72 (blind) dr_date_test(outcome, treatment, covariates, seed = 1)    # p = 0.002 (sees it)

title: Aggregate-Likelihood Downscaling — aggregate_downscale
text: y Numeric vector length dim_y, 1 x dim_y matrix. observed aggregate. aggregator Either numeric dim_y x dim_x matrix (treated linear aggregator dispatched closed-form path) function function(x) -> y_matrix maps n x dim_x matrix latent samples n x dim_y matrix aggregates (non-linear path). latent_prior Either () list elements means (N x dim_x matrix list dim_x-vectors), covariances (list N dim_x x dim_x matrices), weights (length-N numeric, summing 1) – (b) proxymix::gmm_fit (object exposing @means, @covariances, @weights slots). sigma_y Numeric. Observation noise standard deviation (scalar; eps ~ N(0, sigma_y^2 )). Default 0.1. n_samples_per_component Integer. Importance-sampling sample count per prior component (non-linear path ). Default 200L. min_ess_fraction Numeric (0, 1]. ESS-floor reliability gate path: per-component ESS drops min_ess_fraction * n_samples_per_component, warning emitted. Default 0.1. Set 0 disable. seed Integer NULL. Random seed (non-linear path).

title: Aggregate-Likelihood Downscaling — aggregate_downscale
text: object class "aggregate_downscale" components: posterior_mean – length-dim_x numeric, E[X | y]. posterior_cov – dim_x x dim_x matrix, Cov[X | y] (law--total-covariance mixture components). posterior_weights – length-N numeric, posterior mixture weights (sum 1). posterior_components_means – list N length-dim_x posterior component means. posterior_components_covariances – list N posterior component covariances. aggregator_type – "linear" "nonlinear". method – "linear_closed_form" "nonlinear_is". ess_per_component – length-N per-component ESS (path ; NA closed form). ess_warning – TRUE per-component ESS fell floor (path ). n_components, sigma_y, n_samples_per_component, call.

title: Aggregate-Likelihood Downscaling — aggregate_downscale
text: third downscaling method kernR, structurally different existing pair: kernel_downscale() (CME, Park-Muandet-Fukumizu-Sejdinovic 2013) – paired (coarse, fine) training data; supervised regression. dist_regression() (Szabo-Sriperumbudur-Poczos-Gretton 2016) – distribution--distribution mapping bag--points design. aggregate_downscale() (function) – aggregate observations + known aggregator + parametric latent prior. Used paired training data exists (coarse-grid observations) aggregation operator known (spatial averaging, temporal averaging, linear non-linear projection). Two computational paths, selected aggregator's class: Linear-Gaussian closed form (aggregator matrix ): prior component's posterior Kalman update; K_k = Sigma_k ^T (Sigma_k ^T + sigma_y^2 )^{-1}; mu_k|y = mu_k + K_k (y - mu_k), Sigma_k|y = (- K_k ) Sigma_k; posterior mixture weights reweight per-component evidence N(y | mu_k, Sigma_k ^T + sigma_y^2 ). Non-linear importance sampling (aggregator function): draw n_samples_per_component samples prior component, evaluate T(.), weight Gaussian likelihood N(y | T(x), sigma_y^2 ), recover posterior moments + reweighted mixture weights importance-weighted samples. Reports per-component effective sample size; warns stated floor. aggregate-likelihood / GMM-proxy direction shared companion proxymix Tier-2 stub proxymix::from_aggregate_likelihood(), targets problem prior-fitting side; function targets consumption side (inversion given fitted prior).

title: Aggregate-Likelihood Downscaling — aggregate_downscale
code: set.seed(1L) # Linear-Gaussian: spatial averaging of two adjacent cells. A <- matrix(c(0.5, 0.5), nrow = 1L) prior <- list(   means = list(c(0, 0), c(2, 2)),   covariances = list(diag(2L), diag(2L)),   weights = c(0.5, 0.5) ) fit <- aggregate_downscale(y = 1.0, aggregator = A,                            latent_prior = prior, sigma_y = 0.2) fit$posterior_mean #> [1] 1 1  # Non-linear: aggregator is sin of the sum. agg_fn <- function(x) matrix(sin(rowSums(x)), ncol = 1L) fit2 <- aggregate_downscale(y = 0.5, aggregator = agg_fn,                             latent_prior = prior, sigma_y = 0.1,                             n_samples_per_component = 300L, seed = 1L) #> Warning: aggregate_downscale(): per-component IS effective sample size fell below 10% of n_samples_per_component for component(s) 2 (ESS = 27.9). Increase `n_samples_per_component`, sharpen the prior, or consider a coarser aggregator if posterior is locally narrow. fit2$posterior_mean #> [1] 0.8567869 0.8729094

Method density ratio estimation: "logistic" (default), "ranger", "xgboost", "proxymix", "rulsif". "proxymix" backend fits Gaussian-mixture proxies joint product--marginals sample clouds via classical EM (Hoek & Elliott, 2024), giving parametric alternative NCE-based classifiers; useful multimodal densities classifier calibration unreliable. Requires proxymix package (>= 0.3.0). n_permutations Integer. Number permutations null distribution. Default 500. n_clusters Integer "auto". Number propensity clusters valid permutation cluster_id = NULL. Default "auto". split_ratio Numeric (0, 1). Proportion data training density ratio estimator. Default 0.5. alpha Numeric. Significance level. Default 0.05. seed Integer NULL. Random seed reproducibility. verbose Logical. Print progress. Default FALSE. cluster_id Optional vector length nrow(x) identifying external clusters (e.g. site, season, paddock, farm). supplied, permutation null built within-cluster reshuffling y, preserves cluster-level effects null. Coerced factor; test split inherits cluster assignment. result carries per-cluster stratified bd-HSIC alongside pooled statistic. permutation Character. Permutation scheme: "auto" (default) – cluster_id supplied, equivalent "within_cluster"; otherwise falls back k-means clustering propensity weights (original Hu/Sejdinovic/Evans scheme). "within_cluster" – requires cluster_id. Permutes y indices within clusters; preserves cluster-level effects. "naive" – unrestricted permutation across observations. Use independence within clusters plausible (rarely true ag-systems data). min_ess_fraction Numeric (0, 1) 0 / non-finite disable. ESS-floor reliability gate (added 0.0.0.9013): weighted-HSIC effective sample size min_ess_fraction * n_test, warning emitted result$ess_warning TRUE. default 0.1 (10%) conservative floor; tighten studies strict reliability requirements.

title: Backdoor-HSIC Test for Causal Association — bd_hsic_test
text: bd-HSIC test (Hu, Sejdinovic & Evans, 2024) tests whether treatment X causal effect outcome Y adjusting confounders Z via backdoor criterion. test works : Estimating density ratios w(x, z) = p*(x) / p(x|z) reweight observational samples interventional distribution. Computing weighted HSIC statistic X Y. Obtaining p-values via permutation Y within exchangeability clusters – propensity-similarity clusters default, external design clusters (site / season / paddock) cluster_id supplied. Hierarchical extension. design naturally clustered (multi-site agricultural trials, paddock x season factorial designs, patient x hospital data), supplying cluster_id activates within-cluster permutation: indices y reshuffled within cluster, preserving cluster-level effects null. safer default clustered data; naive permutation across clusters can inflate Type error cluster effects exist. Unlike PDS Double ML, bd-HSIC can detect non-linear causal effects (e.g., U-shaped relationships) treatment affects higher moments outcome necessarily mean.

title: Backdoor-HSIC Test for Causal Association — bd_hsic_test
text: density-ratio estimator now fit train split predicted held-test split via fit_density_ratio() + predict_density_ratio(). documented split_ratio honoured end--end four classifier / proxymix backends. 0.0.0.9013 sample-split leak warning therefore retired. RuLSIF, kernel-based closed-form backend, still uses estimate_rulsif() train/test split natively. fitted density-ratio model preserved result$density_ratio_fit callers want backend diagnostics (see ?fit_density_ratio Value; proxymix exposes BIC, AIC, log-likelihood, convergence per GMM).

title: Kernel Distribution Regression — dist_regression
text: Outer kernels supported. "linear": \(K(P_i, P_j) = \langle \hat{\mu}_{P_i},   \hat{\mu}_{P_j} \rangle =   \frac{1}{n_i n_j} \sum_{k,l} k(x_i^{(k)}, x_j^{(l)})\). "rbf": \(K(P_i, P_j) = \exp(-\| \hat{\mu}_{P_i} -   \hat{\mu}_{P_j} \|^2 / (2\sigma^2))\), embedding-space distance recovered inner Gram via \(\|\hat\mu_i - \hat\mu_j\|^2 = G_{ii} - 2 G_{ij} + G_{jj}\). Typical ag-systems use: paddock contributes bag soil-core measurements (variable depth, multiple cores per paddock); regression predicts paddock-level yield distributional shape soil profile. Distinct kernel_downscale() input distribution, fixed-length vector.

title: Kernel Distribution Regression — dist_regression

Muandet, K., Fukumizu, K., Sriperumbudur, B., & Scholkopf, B. (2017). Kernel mean embedding distributions: review beyond. Foundations Trends Machine Learning, 10(1-2).

title: DR-DATE for Two PESTO Ensemble Scenarios — dr_date_scenario
code: # \donttest{ # Requires PESTO (>= 0.4.1) -- wired through Imports. library(PESTO) npar <- 2L; nobs <- 4L; nreal <- 60L G  <- matrix(stats::rnorm(nobs * npar), nobs, npar) y0 <- as.numeric(G %*% c(1.0, -0.5)) + stats::rnorm(nobs, sd = 0.05) y1 <- y0 + c(0.6, 0.6, 0.6, 0.6)   # intervention shifts outputs names(y0) <- names(y1) <- paste0("o", seq_len(nobs))  prior <- matrix(stats::rnorm(nreal * npar), nreal, npar,                 dimnames = list(NULL, c("p1", "p2"))) fit0 <- pesto_ies_callback(function(t) t %*% t(G), prior, y0, 0.05,                            noptmax = 3, verbose = FALSE) fit1 <- pesto_ies_callback(function(t) t %*% t(G), prior, y1, 0.05,                            noptmax = 3, verbose = FALSE) m_base <- as_manifest(fit0, run_id = "baseline") m_intv <- as_manifest(fit1, run_id = "intervention") res <- dr_date_scenario(m_base, m_intv,                          n_permutations = 200L, seed = 1L) print(res) #>  #>    DR-DATE (scenario) Test #>  #> Statistic: 0.203779  #> P-value:   1.0000  #> N:         120  #> Perms:     200  #> Kernel Y:  rbf (bw = 0.8709) #> ESS:       60.0  #>  #> Scenario contrast #>   baseline      : baseline (n=60) #>   intervention  : intervention (n=60) #>   outputs tested: o1, o2, o3, o4 #>   PESTO versions: baseline=0.5.0, intervention=0.5.0 #>   fidelity      : baseline=single, intervention=single #>   Verdict:        fail to reject (no distributional difference detected) #>  # }

title: Doubly Robust Distributional Average Treatment Effect Test (DR-DATE) — dr_date_test
text: DR-DATE test (Fawkes, Hu, Evans & Sejdinovic, 2024) constructs doubly robust (augmented inverse-probability-weighted) estimators counterfactual mean embeddings Y(1) Y(0) reproducing kernel Hilbert space. arm \(\) augmented embedding $$\hat\mu_a = \frac{1}{n}\sum_i \tilde w_{,}   \bigl(k(y_i,\cdot) - \hat m_a(x_i)\bigr) + \hat m_a(x_i),$$ \(\hat m_a\) conditional mean embedding fitted arm \(\) \(\tilde w_{,}\) stabilised inverse-probability weights. statistic \(\|\hat\mu_1 - \hat\mu_0\|^2\) RKHS. Setting outcome_model = "zero" sets \(\hat m_a \equiv 0\) recovers inverse-probability-weighted statistic. Double robustness: test consistent either propensity model outcome (CME) model correctly specified. Cross-fitting (cross_fit = TRUE) makes hold flexible machine-learning nuisances removing -observation overfitting bias (Chernozhukov et al., 2018). Permutation null: reference distribution permutes treatment labels within propensity-score bins, holding fitted nuisances fixed. valid within-bin exchangeability treatment given (binned) propensity score; calibration degrades bins coarsen relative propensity variation inside . Key advantage: unlike DML TMLE test mean shifts, DR-DATE detects distributional difference including changes variance, skewness, shape.

title: Doubly Robust Distributional Average Treatment Effect Test (DR-DATE) — dr_date_test
text: Fawkes, J., Hu, R., Evans, R. J., & Sejdinovic, D. (2024). Doubly robust kernel statistics testing distributional treatment effects. Transactions Machine Learning Research. Chernozhukov, V., Chetverikov, D., Demirer, M., Duflo, E., Hansen, C., Newey, W., & Robins, J. (2018). Double/debiased machine learning treatment structural parameters. Econometrics Journal, 21(1), C1-C68.

title: Doubly Robust Distributional Average Treatment Effect Test (DR-DATE) — dr_date_test
code: set.seed(42) n <- 300 x <- matrix(rnorm(n * 2), n, 2) logit_p <- 0.5 * x[, 1] t <- rbinom(n, 1, plogis(logit_p)) y <- t * 1.0 + x[, 1] + rnorm(n, sd = 0.5)  result <- dr_date_test(y, t, x, n_permutations = 200, seed = 1) print(result) #>  #>    DR-DATE Test #>  #> Statistic: 0.176911  #> P-value:   0.0050  #> N:         300  #> Perms:     200  #> Kernel Y:  rbf (bw = 1.249) #> ESS:       133.7  #>

title: Doubly Robust Distributional Effect on the Treated Test (DR-DETT) — dr_dett_test
text: DR-DETT analogous DR-DATE focuses effect treated (ETT) rather average treatment effect. treated counterfactual Y(1) | T = 1 observed directly, control arm needs outcome model. control counterfactual Y(0) | T = 1 reconstructed augmented inverse-probability weighting, reweighting controls treatment odds \(e(x) / (1 - e(x))\) covariate distribution matches treated. outcome_model = "krr" control conditional mean embedding \(\hat m_0\) supplies doubly robust augmentation; outcome_model = "zero" returns inverse-probability-weighted statistic. requires one-sided overlap: P(T = 1 | X) bounded away 0 (necessarily 1), applies positivity fails control group.

title: Doubly Robust Distributional Effect on the Treated Test (DR-DETT) — dr_dett_test
text: Fawkes, J., Hu, R., Evans, R. J., & Sejdinovic, D. (2024). Doubly robust kernel statistics testing distributional treatment effects. Transactions Machine Learning Research.

title: Doubly Robust Distributional Effect on the Treated Test (DR-DETT) — dr_dett_test
code: set.seed(42) n <- 300 x <- matrix(rnorm(n * 2), n, 2) t <- rbinom(n, 1, plogis(0.5 * x[, 1])) y <- t * rnorm(n, sd = 2) + (1 - t) * rnorm(n, sd = 1) + x[, 1]  result <- dr_dett_test(y, t, x, n_permutations = 200, seed = 1) print(result) #>  #>    DR-DETT Test #>  #> Statistic: 0.0342116  #> P-value:   0.0149  #> N:         300  #> Perms:     200  #> Kernel Y:  rbf (bw = 1.716) #> ESS:       102.2  #>

title: Fit a Density-Ratio Model — fit_density_ratio
text: Four backends supported (method argument): "logistic" (default), "ranger", "xgboost" – classifier-based noise-contrastive estimation. classifier trained distinguish joint samples (x, z) product--marginals samples (x_perm, z); density ratio recovered calibrated class probabilities. Log-ratios stored internally numerical stability. "proxymix" – Gaussian-mixture density-ratio. Fits one GMM joint sample cloud (x, z) one permuted product--marginals cloud via proxymix::fit_proxymix(regime = "sample"); ratios evaluated log-space proxymix::dgmm(). Per-GMM convergence diagnostics (BIC, AIC, final log-likelihood, iteration count) surfaced returned fit; query via fit$diagnostics. Introduced kernR 0.0.0.9014 close documented-- unimplemented sample-split gap bd_hsic_test() (see NEWS). estimate_density_ratio() now thin backwards-compatible wrapper fits predicts data.

text: clustered data (e.g., patients within hospitals, plots within farms), standard kernel tests may inflated type error observations within cluster independent. function decomposes test : Within-cluster: Average within-cluster test statistics (tests treatment effects within cluster). -cluster: Test cluster-level mean embeddings (tests treatment effects across clusters). combined statistic weighted sum, weights determined weight_method. Permutation performed within clusters preserve hierarchical structure.

title: Hierarchical Kernel Causal Test — hierarchical_test
code: # \donttest{ set.seed(42) n_clusters <- 20 n_per <- 30 n <- n_clusters * n_per cluster_id <- rep(1:n_clusters, each = n_per)  # Cluster-level random effects cluster_effect <- rnorm(n_clusters, sd = 1)[cluster_id] x <- matrix(rnorm(n * 2), n, 2) t <- rbinom(n, 1, plogis(0.3 * x[, 1])) y <- 0.5 * t + cluster_effect + x[, 1] + rnorm(n)  result <- hierarchical_test(y, t, x, cluster_id,   method = "dr-date",   n_permutations = 100,   seed = 1 ) print(result) #>  #>    Hierarchical-DRDATE Test #>  #> Statistic: 0.0309451  #> P-value:   0.0099  #> N:         600  #> Perms:     100  #> Kernel Y:  rbf (bw = 1.822) #>  # }

title: HSIC-Based Distributional Sensitivity Index — hsic_sensitivity
text: First-order index (always computed): $$S^{HSIC}_j = \frac{HSIC(X_j, Y)}{\sqrt{HSIC(X_j, X_j) \cdot HSIC(Y, Y)}}$$ direct contribution X_j Y – analogous (equal ) Sobol first-order index. Total-order index (total_order = TRUE): $$T^{HSIC}_j = 1 - \frac{HSIC(X_{\sim j}, Y)}{\sqrt{HSIC(X_{\sim j}, X_{\sim j}) \cdot HSIC(Y, Y)}}$$ X_{~j} parameter design column j removed. construction difference T_j - S_j captures contribution X_j interactions parameters. purely additive models T_j = S_j; presence interaction T_j > S_j. Unlike variance-based Sobol indices, versions HSIC-Sensitivity Index capture non-linear distributional effects: parameter affects variance, skewness, tail Y without shifting mean invisible Sobol visible HSIC. normalisation bounds index [0, 1]. Optional permutation p-values computed first-order indices (Benjamini-Hochberg-adjusted across grid default). Total-order uncertainty quantification (total_order_ci = TRUE, since 0.0.0.9013). pair-bootstrap percentile CI index T_j : resample (theta, y) pairs replacement n_bootstrap times, recompute T_j resample, report ci_level (default 95%) two-sided percentile interval. uncertainty quantification, hypothesis test. Total-order conditional-permutation significance test (total_order_test = "cond_perm", since 0.0.0.9014). Tests H_0: X_j _||_ Y | X_{~j} – null, X_j adds nothing beyond already X_{~j} T_j concentrated zero. null generated conditional permutation: k-means-cluster design points X_{~j} similarity, within cluster permute Y; recompute T_j permuted design. p-value = (1 + #{T_perm >= T_obs}) / (1 + B). p_adjust method applies grid-wide. 0.0.0.9012 total_order_p_value pair-bootstrap implementation – sampled empirical joint, null---effect, retracted 0.0.0.9013. 0.0.0.9014 conditional-permutation test repopulates p_value_total_order properly-calibrated value; total_order_test flag result distinguishes new mode retracted one. Power caveat (empirical calibration 2026-05-16). Repeated-seed calibration (orchestra_calibration_20260516.R, B = 100, N = 80) confirms cond_perm test null-calibrated (per-parameter type-rates 0.01-0.04 nominal alpha = 0.05) conservative additive designs (rejection rate ~3% strong-additive design first-order test rejects 90%). reason structural: additive Y = sum_j f_j(X_j), conditional permutation Y within X_{~j} bins preserves X_{~j}'s contribution randomises X_j component (independent X_{~j}), permuted HSIC(X_{~j}, Y_perm) statistically close observed HSIC(X_{~j}, Y). Use cond_perm interaction detection; use first-order permutation p_value (always computed p_value = TRUE) additive contributions. two complementary, interchangeable. Total-order CIs cost B * p * q kernel re-evaluations resampled designs; set n_bootstrap accordingly. Use CI honest uncertainty bars index magnitude; interpret significance verdict.

what: kernR: Kernel-Based Causal Distributional Testing — kernR-package
title: kernR: Kernel-Based Causal Distributional Testing — kernR-package
text: Kernel-based hypothesis tests causal inference distributional treatment effects. Implements backdoor-adjusted HSIC (bd-HSIC) testing causal association, doubly robust kernel statistics (DR-DATE, DR-DETT) testing distributional treatment effects beyond mean shifts. Supports binary, continuous, mixed treatments, hierarchical/nested data structures, scales large datasets via Nystrom approximation random Fourier features. package depends 'PESTO' (GPL (>= 3)); kernR's sources released MIT licence, installed combination 'PESTO' combined work subject terms GPL (>= 3).

title: Kernel-Based Statistical Downscaling — kernel_downscale
text: Predicts fine-resolution outputs new coarse-resolution inputs via conditional mean embedding (CME) regression RKHS. Given paired coarse-fine training data (coarse, fine), fits operator \(E[Y \mid X = x]\) closed form via kernel ridge regression returns predictions new_coarse. Park-Muandet-Fukumizu-Sejdinovic conditional-mean-embedding scheme, specialised regression form needed spatial / temporal downscaling.

title: Kernel-Based Statistical Downscaling — kernel_downscale
text: Typical ag-systems use: coarse climate-grid inputs (e.g. monthly temperature, rainfall 25 km grid) -> fine-resolution outputs (paddock yield, biomass) time index. Train years coarse fine observed; predict fine outputs new coarse inputs. Compared linear regression baseline, kernel approach captures non-linear coarse-fine relationships without specifying functional form. Compared deep-learning downscalers, closed-form solution, uses orders--magnitude less data, carries interpretable kernel-bandwidth degrees--freedom knob.

title: Kernel Stein Discrepancy Goodness-of-Fit Test — ksd_test
text: KSD score-based, one-sample complement mmd_test(): MMD compares two samples, KSD compares sample density. calibration framing direct – given posterior ensemble draws score distribution claim represent, KSD asks whether draws actually follow distribution. sensitive mean, variance, tail mis-specification. default base kernel inverse multi-quadric (IMQ), \(k(x, y) = (c^2 + \lVert x - y \rVert^2)^\beta\) \(\beta \(-1, 0)\). Gorham & Mackey (2017) show IMQ Stein discrepancy detects non-convergence regimes Gaussian (RBF) Stein discrepancy blind, particularly dimension grows; RBF base kernel remains available via kernel = "rbf". offset c (IMQ) bandwidth h (RBF) default median heuristic sample. Reproducibility: wild-bootstrap multipliers drawn R's RNG, non-NULL seed makes p-value reproducible active RNG kind (R default Mersenne-Twister unless changed caller). exact test materialises n x n Stein-kernel matrix, memory compute scale O(n^2). keep large samples tractable without silent loss exactness, sample n_exact_max rows delegated ksd_test_nystrom() – low-rank approximation announced message recorded returned object's approximation m fields, result stays reproducible object. Set n_exact_max = Inf force exact O(n^2) test size, call ksd_test_nystrom() directly control approximation rank m.

title: Kernel Stein Discrepancy Goodness-of-Fit Test — ksd_test
code: set.seed(1)  # Well-specified: standard-normal sample against standard-normal target x_ok <- matrix(stats::rnorm(400L), ncol = 2L) fit_ok <- ksd_test(x_ok, n_boot = 199L, seed = 1L) fit_ok #>  #>    KSD Test #>  #> Statistic: -0.00225082  #> P-value:   0.6300  #> N:         200  #> Perms:     199  #> Kernel X:  imq #>  #> Goodness-of-fit verdict #>   Stein kernel: imq (beta = -0.5) #>   Bandwidth:    1.608 (median heuristic) #>   Bootstrap:    wild, B = 199 #>   Surprise:     0.667 bits #>   Verdict:      consistent with target #>   # Mis-specified: mean-shifted sample against the same target x_bad <- x_ok + 1 fit_bad <- ksd_test(x_bad, n_boot = 199L, seed = 1L) fit_bad #>  #>    KSD Test #>  #> Statistic: 0.946639  #> P-value:   0.0050  #> N:         200  #> Perms:     199  #> Kernel X:  imq #>  #> Goodness-of-fit verdict #>   Stein kernel: imq (beta = -0.5) #>   Bandwidth:    1.608 (median heuristic) #>   Bootstrap:    wild, B = 199 #>   Surprise:     7.644 bits #>   Verdict:      REJECT (sample inconsistent with target) #>   # Explicit non-standard target via the Gaussian score factory sig <- matrix(c(1, 0.6, 0.6, 1), nrow = 2L) x_cor <- x_ok %*% chol(sig) ksd_test(x_cor, score = gaussian_score(sigma = sig),          n_boot = 199L, seed = 1L) #>  #>    KSD Test #>  #> Statistic: -0.00438649  #> P-value:   0.6050  #> N:         200  #> Perms:     199  #> Kernel X:  imq #>  #> Goodness-of-fit verdict #>   Stein kernel: imq (beta = -0.5) #>   Bandwidth:    1.472 (median heuristic) #>   Bootstrap:    wild, B = 199 #>   Surprise:     0.725 bits #>   Verdict:      consistent with target #>

what: Accelerated Kernel Stein Discrepancy Goodness-of-Fit Test (Nystrom) — ksd_test_nystrom
title: Accelerated Kernel Stein Discrepancy Goodness-of-Fit Test (Nystrom) — ksd_test_nystrom
text: Low-rank counterpart ksd_test() large samples. n x n Stein-kernel matrix never materialised: replaced Nystrom factor F (n x m, m << n) \(F F^\top \approx U\), KSD U-statistic wild-bootstrap null computed F O(n m) rather O(n^2). Langevin Stein kernel positive semi-definite, \(F F^\top\) valid Stein kernel right, exactly ksd_test() procedure applied rank-m kernel \(F F^\top\): wild-bootstrap calibration degenerate U-statistic null preserved, approximation trades statistical power – test validity – speed.

what: Wild bootstrap of the kernel Stein discrepancy null — ksd_wild_bootstrap_cpp
title: Wild bootstrap of the kernel Stein discrepancy null — ksd_wild_bootstrap_cpp
text: Given Stein-kernel matrix H, draws n_boot wild-bootstrap replicates degenerate U-statistic null via independent Rademacher multipliers \(W_i \\{-1, +1\}\): replicate \((1 / (n (n - 1))) \sum_{\ne j} W_i W_j H_{ij}\). diagonal excluded match unbiased U-statistic. Multipliers drawn R's RNG (R::unif_rand within Rcpp's RNGScope), callers honour set.seed().

title: MMD Posterior-Predictive Check — mmd_ppc
text: Model-free verdict whether posterior-predictive ensemble consistent held-observations, via Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) two-sample test. Wraps mmd_test() posterior-predictive framing adds Shannon-information surprise diagnostic (-log2(p)) intuitive interpretation: 0 bits = surprise (p = 1); ~4.32 bits = p = 0.05; maximum achievable surprise n_permutations = B log2(B + 1).

title: MMD Posterior-Predictive Check — mmd_ppc
code: mmd_ppc(x, ...)  # Default S3 method mmd_ppc(   x,   observed,   kernel = kernel_spec(),   n_permutations = 500L,   alpha = 0.05,   seed = NULL,   ... )  # S3 method for class 'pesto_ensemble' mmd_ppc(x, observed = NULL, ...)  # S3 method for class 'pesto_ensemble_manifest' mmd_ppc(x, observed, outputs = NULL, ...)

title: MMD Posterior-Predictive Check — mmd_ppc

Additional arguments (currently unused; reserved future Nystrom acceleration). observed Numeric matrix n_obs x d held-observations. x pesto_ensemble carrying observed slot, may left NULL use bundled observations. kernel Kernel specification. Default RBF median heuristic pooled posterior + observed sample. n_permutations Integer. Permutations null. Default 500. alpha Numeric (0, 1). Significance level used verdict. Default 0.05. seed Integer NULL. Random seed reproducibility. outputs Optional character vector output column names manifest test . Defaults numeric output columns (real_name column excluded). Used pesto_ensemble_manifest method.

MMD test sensitive mean, variance, tail differences – strictly informative RMSE posterior predictive mean. pesto_ensemble_manifest method records provenance input manifest result$pesto_metadata: run_id, pesto_version, method, outputs_used, fidelity (multi-fidelity provenance record, NULL single-fidelity run), PPC verdict traces back producing ensemble fidelity calibrated .

title: MMD Posterior-Predictive Check — mmd_ppc
text: Gretton, ., Borgwardt, K. M., Rasch, M. J., Scholkopf, B., & Smola, . (2012). kernel two-sample test. JMLR, 13, 723-773. Gelman, ., Meng, X.-L., & Stern, H. (1996). Posterior predictive assessment model fitness via realized discrepancies. Statistica Sinica, 6(4), 733-760.

title: MMD Posterior-Predictive Check — mmd_ppc
code: set.seed(1) # Calibrated model: posterior matches truth post <- matrix(stats::rnorm(400L), ncol = 2L) obs  <- matrix(stats::rnorm(40L),  ncol = 2L) fit_ok <- mmd_ppc(post, obs, n_permutations = 199L, seed = 1L) fit_ok #>  #>    MMD PPC Test #>  #> Statistic: -0.0113161  #> P-value:   0.7600  #> N:         220  #> Perms:     199  #> Kernel X:  rbf (bw = 1.619) #>  #> PPC verdict #>   Posterior:  200 draws #>   Observed:   20 obs #>   Surprise:   0.396 bits #>   Verdict:    consistent with observations #>   # Miscalibrated model: posterior is mean-shifted obs_shift <- obs + 1.5 fit_bad <- mmd_ppc(post, obs_shift, n_permutations = 199L, seed = 1L) fit_bad #>  #>    MMD PPC Test #>  #> Statistic: 0.373352  #> P-value:   0.0050  #> N:         220  #> Perms:     199  #> Kernel X:  rbf (bw =  1.72) #>  #> PPC verdict #>   Posterior:  200 draws #>   Observed:   20 obs #>   Surprise:   7.644 bits #>   Verdict:    REJECT (posterior inconsistent with observations) #>

text: function one argument (n x d numeric matrix) returning n x d matrix finite-difference scores, suitable score argument ksd_test().

title: Finite-difference score from a log-density — numeric_score
text: makes ksd_test() usable target user can write log density, targets hand-derived score. also kernR-side adapter log-posterior contract: wrapping exported log-posterior evaluator numeric_score() yields score ksd_test() needs check whether posterior draws calibrated posterior, dependency added producer – evaluator passed ordinary function. Central differences cost 2 * d evaluations log_density per call, d dimension. cheap closed-form target prefer gaussian_score() hand-written score; numeric_score() general fallback.

title: Finite-difference score from a log-density — numeric_score
text: Max Moldovan, max.moldovan@adelaide.edu.au

title: Finite-difference score from a log-density — numeric_score
code: set.seed(1) x <- matrix(stats::rnorm(200L), ncol = 2L)  # Standard-normal log density (unnormalised): -||x||^2 / 2 ld <- function(z) -0.5 * rowSums(z^2) s <- numeric_score(ld)  # Matches the closed-form standard-normal score -x to finite-difference order max(abs(s(x) - (-x))) #> [1] 5.821565e-12  # Use directly in a goodness-of-fit test ksd_test(x, score = numeric_score(ld), n_boot = 199L, seed = 1L) #>  #>    KSD Test #>  #> Statistic: 7.48743e-05  #> P-value:   0.4000  #> N:         100  #> Perms:     199  #> Kernel X:  imq #>  #> Goodness-of-fit verdict #>   Stein kernel: imq (beta = -0.5) #>   Bandwidth:     1.57 (median heuristic) #>   Bootstrap:    wild, B = 199 #>   Surprise:     1.322 bits #>   Verdict:      consistent with target #>

title: Nystrom Low-Rank Kernel Factorisation — nystrom_factor
text: construction : Sample m landmark indices uniformly without replacement. Compute landmark Gram \(W = K_{mm}\) (m x m) cross-Gram \(C = K_{nm}\) (n x m). Stabilise: \(W_\epsilon = W + \epsilon \). Cholesky factor \(W_\epsilon = L L^\top\). Return \(F = C L^{-\top}\), \(F F^\top = C W_\epsilon^{-1} C^\top \approx K\). kernel_spec() supported. Bandwidth selection (median heuristic) performed full dataset landmark sampling.

title: Nystrom Low-Rank Kernel Factorisation — nystrom_factor
text: Williams, C. K. ., & Seeger, M. (2001). Using Nystrom method speed kernel machines. NeurIPS, 13.

title: Nystrom Low-Rank Kernel Factorisation — nystrom_factor
code: set.seed(1) x <- matrix(stats::rnorm(2000L), ncol = 2L) f <- nystrom_factor(x, m = 80L, seed = 1L) dim(f$F) #> [1] 1000   80

title: kernR 0.7.0
text: New concordance_test_nystrom(): low-rank counterpart concordance_test() large ensembles. pooled sample factorised – Nystrom method (default) random Fourier features – summed pairwise unbiased MMD joint-permutation null computed n x m factor O(n m) per permutation rather O(n^2), rank m controlling speed / accuracy trade-. verdict object pairwise discrepancy matrix localises offending source identical exact test. New ksd_test_nystrom(): low-rank counterpart ksd_test() large samples. n x n Stein-kernel matrix never materialised; replaced Nystrom factor kernel Stein discrepancy wild-bootstrap null computed O(n m). Langevin Stein kernel positive semi-definite, factorised kernel valid Stein kernel right, accelerated test exact procedure applied rank-m kernel: degenerate-U-statistic calibration preserved (empirical Type-error stays nominal level), approximation trades statistical power – test validity – speed. ksd_test() concordance_test() gain n_exact_max argument (default 5000). sample-size ceiling call delegated low-rank counterpart, large sample stays tractable without O(n^2) blow-. switch never silent: announced message, recorded returned object (approximation m, result reproducible object), escapable n_exact_max = Inf force exact test size.

title: kernR 0.6.0
text: New coverage_test(): graded coverage / calibration diagnostic predictive ensemble held-observations. mmd_ppc() ksd_test() give binary reject/accept verdict, coverage_test() reports way ensemble mis-calibrated – empirical coverage nominal interval levels, signed dispersion ratio (Var(PIT)/(1/12): one -dispersion / -confidence, one -dispersion), bias indicator, rank-histogram uniformity test – classifying ensemble calibrated, -dispersed, -dispersed, biased. interpretable companion kernel verdicts, quantifies ensemble--dispersion cross-member PESTO validation surfaced (e.g. “90% intervals cover 56% – -dispersed factor 1.9”). Dispatches numeric matrix, pesto_ensemble, pesto_ensemble_manifest (threading fidelity provenance), mirroring mmd_ppc(). Marginal (per-output) calibration; pure R.
